Application Streams will adhere to the errata criteria for the 10 year life cycle of the major release. Similarly, no new Application Streams will be released during the Maintenance Support Phase and Extended Life Phase since Software Enhancements are not permitted during these phases. However, to achieve the 10 years support criteria for RHEL, one version of each Application Stream will be identified at the start of the Maintenance Support Phase and that version will receive maintenance as described by the support phases. **SMBv1 no longer installed with latest Microsoft Windows 10 and 2016 update (version 1709)**
Type: Article
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/articles/3164551

Content: SMBv1 no longer installed with latest Microsoft Windows 10 and 2016 update (version 1709) Updated 8 Mar 2018 Contents Introduction Resolution Use Cases References Introduction Microsoft recently announced 1 that they will not install SMBv1 by default with the next Redstone 3 (version 1709) update for Windows 10 and Windows 2016. While Windows 2012 R2 still ships SMBv1, Microsoft recommends to disable it as it is becoming increasingly targeted with vulnerabilities and man in the middle attacks. The Content from blogs.technet.microsoft.com is not included. security configuration baseline settings for Windows 10 Redstone 2 also recommends to disable SMBv1. The update release is expected to ship in fall 2017. Server Message Block version 1 (SMBv1) is a 30-year-old network-oriented protocol used in Windows and Active Directory implementations and Microsoft customers have been advised 2 for a long time already to upgrade to a newer version,either SMBv2 or SMBv3. While this change will not have a major impact for Microsoft customers, it does have an impact for Red Hat customers operating their systems in a mixed environment. ... With Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 already in Maintenance Support 2 Phase and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 in Extended Life Phase only, new features, such as the SMBv2/v3 protocol, can not be included. Additionally, Red Hat's ABI stability policy means it is not feasible to backport the current Samba features as well as Linux kernel cifs.ko module. Resolution Red Hat recommends to upgrade to the following versions, at a minimum, to utilize the SMBv2 protocol. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7.2 samba-4.2 It is recommended to upgrade to the most current Red Hat Enterprise Linux release to leverage all the new features and security enhancements. Content: SMBv1 no longer installed with latest Microsoft Windows 10 and 2016 update (version 1709) Updated 8 Mar 2018 Contents Introduction Resolution Use Cases References Introduction Microsoft recently announced 1 that they will not install SMBv1 by default with the next Redstone 3 (version 1709) update for Windows 10 and Windows 2016. While Windows 2012 R2 still ships SMBv1, Microsoft recommends to disable it as it is becoming increasingly targeted with vulnerabilities and man in the middle attacks. The Content from blogs.technet.microsoft.com is not included. security configuration baseline settings for Windows 10 Redstone 2 also recommends to disable SMBv1. The update release is expected to ship in fall 2017. Server Message Block version 1 (SMBv1) is a 30-year-old network-oriented protocol used in Windows and Active Directory implementations and Microsoft customers have been advised 2 for a long time already to upgrade to a newer version,either SMBv2 or SMBv3. While this change will not have a major impact for Microsoft customers, it does have an impact for Red Hat customers operating their systems in a mixed environment. ... With Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 already in Maintenance Support 2 Phase and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 in Extended Life Phase only, new features, such as the SMBv2/v3 protocol, can not be included. Additionally, Red Hat's ABI stability policy means it is not feasible to backport the current Samba features as well as Linux kernel cifs.ko module. Resolution Red Hat recommends to upgrade to the following versions, at a minimum, to utilize the SMBv2 protocol. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7.2 samba-4.2 It is recommended to upgrade to the most current Red Hat Enterprise Linux release to leverage all the new features and security enhancements. Red Hat offers an inplace upgrade from RHEL 6 to RHEL 7; for details, see How do I upgrade from Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 to Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 KBase in the Red Hat Customer Portal. If an upgrade is not an option at this point in time, then customers are advised to delay the installation of the 1709 update until plans to move to RHEL 7 plans are feasible. Use Cases The following use-cases will no longer work on RHEL-6.x and RHEL-7.0/RHEL-7.1 systems: Linux as file sharing client Linux clients will no longer be able to access shares on a Microsoft server when SMBv1 has been removed or disabled. Content: SMBv1 no longer installed with latest Microsoft Windows 10 and 2016 update (version 1709) Updated 8 Mar 2018 Contents Introduction Resolution Use Cases References Introduction Microsoft recently announced 1 that they will not install SMBv1 by default with the next Redstone 3 (version 1709) update for Windows 10 and Windows 2016. While Windows 2012 R2 still ships SMBv1, Microsoft recommends to disable it as it is becoming increasingly targeted with vulnerabilities and man in the middle attacks. The Content from blogs.technet.microsoft.com is not included. security configuration baseline settings for Windows 10 Redstone 2 also recommends to disable SMBv1. The update release is expected to ship in fall 2017. Server Message Block version 1 (SMBv1) is a 30-year-old network-oriented protocol used in Windows and Active Directory implementations and Microsoft customers have been advised 2 for a long time already to upgrade to a newer version,either SMBv2 or SMBv3. While this change will not have a major impact for Microsoft customers, it does have an impact for Red Hat customers operating their systems in a mixed environment. ... With Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 already in Maintenance Support 2 Phase and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 in Extended Life Phase only, new features, such as the SMBv2/v3 protocol, can not be included. Additionally, Red Hat's ABI stability policy means it is not feasible to backport the current Samba features as well as Linux kernel cifs.ko module. Resolution Red Hat recommends to upgrade to the following versions, at a minimum, to utilize the SMBv2 protocol. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7.2 samba-4.2 It is recommended to upgrade to the most current Red Hat Enterprise Linux release to leverage all the new features and security enhancements. Red Hat offers an inplace upgrade from RHEL 6 to RHEL 7; for details, see How do I upgrade from Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 to Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 KBase in the Red Hat Customer Portal. If an upgrade is not an option at this point in time, then customers are advised to delay the installation of the 1709 update until plans to move to RHEL 7 plans are feasible. Use Cases The following use-cases will no longer work on RHEL-6.x and RHEL-7.0/RHEL-7.1 systems: Linux as file sharing client Linux clients will no longer be able to access shares on a Microsoft server when SMBv1 has been removed or disabled. While Windows 2012 R2 still ships SMBv1, Microsoft recommends to disable it as it is becoming increasingly targeted with vulnerabilities and man in the middle attacks. The Content from blogs.technet.microsoft.com is not included. security configuration baseline settings for Windows 10 Redstone 2 also recommends to disable SMBv1. The update release is expected to ship in fall 2017. Server Message Block version 1 (SMBv1) is a 30-year-old network-oriented protocol used in Windows and Active Directory implementations and Microsoft customers have been advised 2 for a long time already to upgrade to a newer version,either SMBv2 or SMBv3. While this change will not have a major impact for Microsoft customers, it does have an impact for Red Hat customers operating their systems in a mixed environment. ... With Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 already in Maintenance Support 2 Phase and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 in Extended Life Phase only, new features, such as the SMBv2/v3 protocol, can not be included. Additionally, Red Hat's ABI stability policy means it is not feasible to backport the current Samba features as well as Linux kernel cifs.ko module. Resolution Red Hat recommends to upgrade to the following versions, at a minimum, to utilize the SMBv2 protocol. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7.2 samba-4.2 It is recommended to upgrade to the most current Red Hat Enterprise Linux release to leverage all the new features and security enhancements. Red Hat offers an inplace upgrade from RHEL 6 to RHEL 7; for details, see How do I upgrade from Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 to Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 KBase in the Red Hat Customer Portal. If an upgrade is not an option at this point in time, then customers are advised to delay the installation of the 1709 update until plans to move to RHEL 7 plans are feasible. Use Cases The following use-cases will no longer work on RHEL-6.x and RHEL-7.0/RHEL-7.1 systems: Linux as file sharing client Linux clients will no longer be able to access shares on a Microsoft server when SMBv1 has been removed or disabled. This file is no longer available on RHEL 7 and later, but the modules.alias file can be a useful replacement. Caution! To determine if full support is available for a specific device, beyond just being recognized by the driver, check the set of release notes for the major version in question as to additional support qualifications. Under rare circumstances, some devices are included within drivers to allow unsupported recognition and use of specific devices for the convenience of our customers. For example, in RHEL 8.4 certain LSI storage controllers 1000:0071 was added back into the megaraid_sas driver, but per the prior Considerations in adopting RHEL 8, "11.1.2. Removed adapters" , these device is still unsupported. In addition, some devices recognized by a driver or some drivers themselves may only be in technical preview . Caution! As of 8.4 and later kernel versions, the modinfo alias pci output information for storage drivers is no longer sufficient by itself to determine specific device support status within the lpfc, qla*, megaraid_sas , mpt3sas , and aacraid drivers. ... These are output as a [VVVV:DDDD] hexadecimal value set when you use the lspci -nnnv command. The kernel uses that information to find a driver that supports that specific VID:DID pair. So the basic methodology to determine if a specific PCI card is recognized by a specific kernel version is a two step process: Method 1: Determine if driver has already been assigned Step 1: Use lspci -nnnvk and check card's output block for a driver: being present See " How to determine the make and model of HBA and version of associated driver from a RHEL Server? Content: SMBv1 no longer installed with latest Microsoft Windows 10 and 2016 update (version 1709) Updated 8 Mar 2018 Contents Introduction Resolution Use Cases References Introduction Microsoft recently announced 1 that they will not install SMBv1 by default with the next Redstone 3 (version 1709) update for Windows 10 and Windows 2016. While Windows 2012 R2 still ships SMBv1, Microsoft recommends to disable it as it is becoming increasingly targeted with vulnerabilities and man in the middle attacks. The Content from blogs.technet.microsoft.com is not included. security configuration baseline settings for Windows 10 Redstone 2 also recommends to disable SMBv1. The update release is expected to ship in fall 2017. Server Message Block version 1 (SMBv1) is a 30-year-old network-oriented protocol used in Windows and Active Directory implementations and Microsoft customers have been advised 2 for a long time already to upgrade to a newer version,either SMBv2 or SMBv3. While this change will not have a major impact for Microsoft customers, it does have an impact for Red Hat customers operating their systems in a mixed environment. ... With Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 already in Maintenance Support 2 Phase and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 in Extended Life Phase only, new features, such as the SMBv2/v3 protocol, can not be included. Additionally, Red Hat's ABI stability policy means it is not feasible to backport the current Samba features as well as Linux kernel cifs.ko module. Resolution Red Hat recommends to upgrade to the following versions, at a minimum, to utilize the SMBv2 protocol. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7.2 samba-4.2 It is recommended to upgrade to the most current Red Hat Enterprise Linux release to leverage all the new features and security enhancements. Red Hat offers an inplace upgrade from RHEL 6 to RHEL 7; for details, see How do I upgrade from Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 to Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 KBase in the Red Hat Customer Portal. If an upgrade is not an option at this point in time, then customers are advised to delay the installation of the 1709 update until plans to move to RHEL 7 plans are feasible. Use Cases The following use-cases will no longer work on RHEL-6.x and RHEL-7.0/RHEL-7.1 systems: Linux as file sharing client Linux clients will no longer be able to access shares on a Microsoft server when SMBv1 has been removed or disabled. Content: SMBv1 no longer installed with latest Microsoft Windows 10 and 2016 update (version 1709) Updated 8 Mar 2018 Contents Introduction Resolution Use Cases References Introduction Microsoft recently announced 1 that they will not install SMBv1 by default with the next Redstone 3 (version 1709) update for Windows 10 and Windows 2016. While Windows 2012 R2 still ships SMBv1, Microsoft recommends to disable it as it is becoming increasingly targeted with vulnerabilities and man in the middle attacks. The Content from blogs.technet.microsoft.com is not included. security configuration baseline settings for Windows 10 Redstone 2 also recommends to disable SMBv1. The update release is expected to ship in fall 2017. Server Message Block version 1 (SMBv1) is a 30-year-old network-oriented protocol used in Windows and Active Directory implementations and Microsoft customers have been advised 2 for a long time already to upgrade to a newer version,either SMBv2 or SMBv3. While this change will not have a major impact for Microsoft customers, it does have an impact for Red Hat customers operating their systems in a mixed environment. ... With Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 already in Maintenance Support 2 Phase and Red Hat Enterprise Linux 5 in Extended Life Phase only, new features, such as the SMBv2/v3 protocol, can not be included. Additionally, Red Hat's ABI stability policy means it is not feasible to backport the current Samba features as well as Linux kernel cifs.ko module. Resolution Red Hat recommends to upgrade to the following versions, at a minimum, to utilize the SMBv2 protocol. Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7.2 samba-4.2 It is recommended to upgrade to the most current Red Hat Enterprise Linux release to leverage all the new features and security enhancements. Red Hat offers an inplace upgrade from RHEL 6 to RHEL 7; for details, see How do I upgrade from Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 to Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 KBase in the Red Hat Customer Portal. If an upgrade is not an option at this point in time, then customers are advised to delay the installation of the 1709 update until plans to move to RHEL 7 plans are feasible. Use Cases The following use-cases will no longer work on RHEL-6.x and RHEL-7.0/RHEL-7.1 systems: Linux as file sharing client Linux clients will no longer be able to access shares on a Microsoft server when SMBv1 has been removed or disabled. Why server is not able to use mand mount option in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8? mand mount option can be used in RHEL 7 and 9 but it is giving error in RHEL8. Resolution Mandatory locking is deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 or later . ... Mandatory locking causes the kernel to check every open, read, and write to verify that the calling process isn’t violating a lock on the given file. To enable mandatory locking in Linux, you need to enable it on a file system level, and also on the individual files. The steps to be followed are: Mount the file system with mand option. For the lock_file, turn on the set-group-ID bit and turn off the group-execute bit, to enable mandatory locking on that particular file. (This way has been chosen because when group-execute bit is turned on, set-group-ID has no real meaning to it) Root Cause To enable mandatory locking in Linux, it has to be enabled at file system level, and also on the individual files. The mand option is deprecated in RHEL8 or later. ... See BUGS below. Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Content: How to enable Mandatory Locking using "mand" option Solution Verified - Updated 29 May 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue How to enable Mandatory Locking using mand option in Red Hat Enterprise Linux? Why server is not able to use mand mount option in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8? mand mount option can be used in RHEL 7 and 9 but it is giving error in RHEL8. Resolution Mandatory locking is deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 or later . ... Mandatory locking causes the kernel to check every open, read, and write to verify that the calling process isn’t violating a lock on the given file. To enable mandatory locking in Linux, you need to enable it on a file system level, and also on the individual files. The steps to be followed are: Mount the file system with mand option. For the lock_file, turn on the set-group-ID bit and turn off the group-execute bit, to enable mandatory locking on that particular file. (This way has been chosen because when group-execute bit is turned on, set-group-ID has no real meaning to it) Root Cause To enable mandatory locking in Linux, it has to be enabled at file system level, and also on the individual files. The mand option is deprecated in RHEL8 or later. ... See BUGS below. Because of these bugs, and the fact that the feature is believed to be little used, since Linux 4.5, mandatory locking has been made an optional feature, governed by a configuration option (CON‐ FIG_MANDATORY_FILE_LOCKING). This feature is no longer supported at all in Linux 5.15 and above. 11.1. File systems of Considerations in adopting RHEL 9 describes the feature as below. Mandatory file locking support has been removed from RHEL 9Mandatory file locking is no longer supported in RHEL 9 and later versions. The mand mount option is ignored by this kernel and its use generates a warning in the system log. The mand option does work in RHEL7. Why server is not able to use mand mount option in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8? mand mount option can be used in RHEL 7 and 9 but it is giving error in RHEL8. Resolution Mandatory locking is deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 or later . ... Mandatory locking causes the kernel to check every open, read, and write to verify that the calling process isn’t violating a lock on the given file. To enable mandatory locking in Linux, you need to enable it on a file system level, and also on the individual files. The steps to be followed are: Mount the file system with mand option. For the lock_file, turn on the set-group-ID bit and turn off the group-execute bit, to enable mandatory locking on that particular file. (This way has been chosen because when group-execute bit is turned on, set-group-ID has no real meaning to it) Root Cause To enable mandatory locking in Linux, it has to be enabled at file system level, and also on the individual files. The mand option is deprecated in RHEL8 or later. ... See BUGS below. Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Why server is not able to use mand mount option in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8? mand mount option can be used in RHEL 7 and 9 but it is giving error in RHEL8. Resolution Mandatory locking is deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 or later . ... Mandatory locking causes the kernel to check every open, read, and write to verify that the calling process isn’t violating a lock on the given file. To enable mandatory locking in Linux, you need to enable it on a file system level, and also on the individual files. The steps to be followed are: Mount the file system with mand option. For the lock_file, turn on the set-group-ID bit and turn off the group-execute bit, to enable mandatory locking on that particular file. (This way has been chosen because when group-execute bit is turned on, set-group-ID has no real meaning to it) Root Cause To enable mandatory locking in Linux, it has to be enabled at file system level, and also on the individual files. The mand option is deprecated in RHEL8 or later. ... See BUGS below. Because of these bugs, and the fact that the feature is believed to be little used, since Linux 4.5, mandatory locking has been made an optional feature, governed by a configuration option (CON‐ FIG_MANDATORY_FILE_LOCKING). This feature is no longer supported at all in Linux 5.15 and above. 11.1. File systems of Considerations in adopting RHEL 9 describes the feature as below. Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Why server is not able to use mand mount option in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8? mand mount option can be used in RHEL 7 and 9 but it is giving error in RHEL8. Resolution Mandatory locking is deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 or later . ... Mandatory locking causes the kernel to check every open, read, and write to verify that the calling process isn’t violating a lock on the given file. To enable mandatory locking in Linux, you need to enable it on a file system level, and also on the individual files. The steps to be followed are: Mount the file system with mand option. For the lock_file, turn on the set-group-ID bit and turn off the group-execute bit, to enable mandatory locking on that particular file. (This way has been chosen because when group-execute bit is turned on, set-group-ID has no real meaning to it) Root Cause To enable mandatory locking in Linux, it has to be enabled at file system level, and also on the individual files. The mand option is deprecated in RHEL8 or later. ... See BUGS below. Because of these bugs, and the fact that the feature is believed to be little used, since Linux 4.5, mandatory locking has been made an optional feature, governed by a configuration option (CON‐ FIG_MANDATORY_FILE_LOCKING). This feature is no longer supported at all in Linux 5.15 and above. 11.1. File systems of Considerations in adopting RHEL 9 describes the feature as below. Mandatory file locking support has been removed from RHEL 9Mandatory file locking is no longer supported in RHEL 9 and later versions. Content: How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10 without xvfb? Solution Verified - Updated 6 Oct 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to run X11 applications in a headless X server on RHEL10? xvfb is longer available on RHEL 10, is there a replacement for it? Resolution Even though Xorg is no longer available, the X11 protocol will remain supported on RHEL 10 via Xwayland. Install xwayland tools $ sudo dnf install xwayland-run.noarch To start a X11 application $ xwfb-run -- <command> It is possible to pass arguments to the X server by using the '-s' option. Each argument must be escaped with two backslashes, for example: $ xwfb-run -- -s \\-ac <command> Root Cause After having been deprecated in RHEL 9, the Xorg server has been removed from RHEL 10, leaving only Wayland. Content: How to enable Mandatory Locking using "mand" option Solution Verified - Updated 29 May 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 7 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue How to enable Mandatory Locking using mand option in Red Hat Enterprise Linux? Why server is not able to use mand mount option in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8? mand mount option can be used in RHEL 7 and 9 but it is giving error in RHEL8. Resolution Mandatory locking is deprecated in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 or later . ... Mandatory locking causes the kernel to check every open, read, and write to verify that the calling process isn’t violating a lock on the given file. To enable mandatory locking in Linux, you need to enable it on a file system level, and also on the individual files. The steps to be followed are: Mount the file system with mand option. For the lock_file, turn on the set-group-ID bit and turn off the group-execute bit, to enable mandatory locking on that particular file. (This way has been chosen because when group-execute bit is turned on, set-group-ID has no real meaning to it) Root Cause To enable mandatory locking in Linux, it has to be enabled at file system level, and also on the individual files. The mand option is deprecated in RHEL8 or later. ... See BUGS below. Because of these bugs, and the fact that the feature is believed to be little used, since Linux 4.5, mandatory locking has been made an optional feature, governed by a configuration option (CON‐ FIG_MANDATORY_FILE_LOCKING). This feature is no longer supported at all in Linux 5.15 and above. 11.1. File systems of Considerations in adopting RHEL 9 describes the feature as below. Mandatory file locking support has been removed from RHEL 9Mandatory file locking is no longer supported in RHEL 9 and later versions. The mand mount option is ignored by this kernel and its use generates a warning in the system log. The mand option does work in RHEL7. Diagnostic Steps File system can be mounted with mand option in RHEL7 and 9 but it is not getting mounted in RHEL8 as option mand is deprecated in RHEL8. In RHEL9, the mand option can be ginve, but is ignored. Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions.

Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions.

Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions.

Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions.

Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions.

**Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7001436
Last updated: 2024-06-13
Content: Cannot create a new RHDS instance when using custom directories Solution Verified - Updated 13 Jun 2024 Environment Red Hat Directory Server 12Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9Red Hat Directory Server 11Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Issue The RHDS allows to setup instances using .inf setup file where configuration parameters can be specified explicitly instead of using an interactive setup. This is useful when setting up multiple instances or automating instance setup tasks. One of the advanced configuration parameters that can be specified is config_dir which is supposed to allow setting the instance main configuration directory. However setting the config_dir parameter to anything other than the default standard location results in the following error during the instance setup ERROR: Error: Instance failed to install, does not exist when expected Resolution Do not use the config_dir parameter. Also, when using # dscreate create-template --advanced to generate a template .inf file with advanced parameters please be aware that changing the advanced options may make your instance install fail . Root Cause The config_dir is deprecated and no longer works and it will be removed in the future RHDS versions.

Red Hat works to enable compatibility between a range of major versions of RHEL (as described above), providing customers the operational flexibility to run a range of container images and hosts in production environments. These combinations extend many applications’ useful life cycles and ease upgrade concerns. Red Hat Support may provide differentiated support between “fully compatible” and “workload specific” configurations (described below). Please discuss this with your support associate. Guidelines for Container Compatibility In specific use cases, Red Hat recommends aligning the major version of the RHEL-based container image and the RHEL container host. Red Hat also recommends following the Red Hat Container Support Policy because other container engines and runtimes are not supported. In RHEL versions 8 and later, Red Hat provides container images for each of the supported architectures (Intel/Amd x86, Arm AArch64, IBM POWER, and zSeries). In general, the correct hardware architecture will automatically be detected, and the correct image will be used. ... The container image and host architectures must match for container images to run properly and be supported. The container host’s hardware must also meet the minimum hardware requirements for the container image in order to run the image correctly (e.g. RHEL 9 container images for IBM POWER LE require POWER9 hardware, RHEL 10 container images for x86_64 require x86-64-v3). There are known limitations when running a newer container image on an older container host and while this configuration often works, such a configuration can introduce additional risk and compatibility problems. If customers run into these limitations, Red Hat recommends upgrading the container host. When containers run with This content is not included. privilege (ex. podman run --privileged) and/or modify the underlying host, Red Hat only supports matched major versions between the container image and container host (ex. RHEL 10 image on a RHEL 10 host). This recommendation also applies when containers are granted security privileges to interact with other containers for the purposes of inspection or monitoring. Aligning the major versions of the image and host ensures the highest degree of compatibility and eliminates many of the edge-cases that users encounter when mixing different versions of container images (userspace) with container hosts (kernels). Finally, customers may run into challenges when running extremely old images on newer container hosts (ex. RHEL 6 container image on RHEL 9 or 10 host). In these situations Red Hat recommends using a version supported in the table above (e.g. RHEL 8, RHEL 9 or RHEL 10 image on a RHEL 9 host.). Resolution Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 This issue is being tracked with the issue This content is not included. RHEL-64281 ( Create LEAPP actor for Pacemaker 9 -> 10 upgrades ) for RHEL 9. Root Cause When doing a leapp upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 there is no warning or upgraded is prevented from proceeding forward when there are pacemaker resources configured that are no longer supported on RHEL 10 or no longer included in RHEL 10 controld , Filesystem resources for gfs2 filesystems. query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions. What would you like to know?

To install a package on a system using `rpm-ostree`, you typically use the `rpm-ostree install` command. This command layers the specified package on top of the base operating system image.

For example, to install the `htop` package, you would use:

```bash
rpm-ostree install htop
```

After running this command, a new deployment is created, and you will need to reboot your system for the changes to take effect:

```bash
systemctl reboot
```

It's important to note that `rpm-ostree` works by creating immutable deployments. When you install a package, you are essentially creating a new, customized operating system image. This differs from traditional package management with `dnf` or `yum` on a mutable system, where changes are applied directly to the running system.

If you need to make more extensive changes or build a custom image, consider using tools like `bootc` for creating container-native bootable OS images.

rpm-ostree CAN install packages — it layers them on top of the base image.
Key facts:
- Correct: rpm-ostree install <package> (e.g., rpm-ostree install tmux)
- A reboot is required to activate the new deployment with the package
- rpm-ostree override and rpm-ostree uninstall are related commands

metric_metadata: {"description": "How relevant the response is to the question", "default": true}
query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions. What would you like to know?

To install a package on a system using `rpm-ostree`, you typically use the `rpm-ostree install` command. This command layers the specified package on top of the base operating system image.

For example, to install the `htop` package, you would use:

```bash
rpm-ostree install htop
```

After running this command, a new deployment is created, and you will need to reboot your system for the changes to take effect:

```bash
systemctl reboot
```

It's important to note that `rpm-ostree` works by creating immutable deployments. When you install a package, you are essentially creating a new, customized operating system image. This differs from traditional package management with `dnf` or `yum` on a mutable system, where changes are applied directly to the running system.

If you need to make more extensive changes or build a custom image, consider using tools like `bootc` for creating container-native bootable OS images.

query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions. What would you like to know?

To install a package on a system using `rpm-ostree`, you typically use the `rpm-ostree install` command. This command layers the specified package on top of the base operating system image.

For example, to install the `htop` package, you would use:

```bash
rpm-ostree install htop
```

After running this command, a new deployment is created, and you will need to reboot your system for the changes to take effect:

```bash
systemctl reboot
```

It's important to note that `rpm-ostree` works by creating immutable deployments. When you install a package, you are essentially creating a new, customized operating system image. This differs from traditional package management with `dnf` or `yum` on a mutable system, where changes are applied directly to the running system.

If you need to make more extensive changes or build a custom image, consider using tools like `bootc` for creating container-native bootable OS images.

Alternatively, the libdb package is available in the unsupported EPEL 10 repository. query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions. What would you like to know?

To install a package on a system using `rpm-ostree`, you typically use the `rpm-ostree install` command. This command layers the specified package on top of the base operating system image.

For example, to install the `htop` package, you would use:

```bash
rpm-ostree install htop
```

After running this command, a new deployment is created, and you will need to reboot your system for the changes to take effect:

```bash
systemctl reboot
```

It's important to note that `rpm-ostree` works by creating immutable deployments. When you install a package, you are essentially creating a new, customized operating system image. This differs from traditional package management with `dnf` or `yum` on a mutable system, where changes are applied directly to the running system.

If you need to make more extensive changes or build a custom image, consider using tools like `bootc` for creating container-native bootable OS images.

query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions. What would you like to know?

To install a package on a system using `rpm-ostree`, you typically use the `rpm-ostree install` command. This command layers the specified package on top of the base operating system image.

For example, to install the `htop` package, you would use:

```bash
rpm-ostree install htop
```

After running this command, a new deployment is created, and you will need to reboot your system for the changes to take effect:

```bash
systemctl reboot
```

It's important to note that `rpm-ostree` works by creating immutable deployments. When you install a package, you are essentially creating a new, customized operating system image. This differs from traditional package management with `dnf` or `yum` on a mutable system, where changes are applied directly to the running system.

If you need to make more extensive changes or build a custom image, consider using tools like `bootc` for creating container-native bootable OS images.

query: How do I install a package on a system using rpm-ostree?
response:
I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions. What would you like to know?

To install a package on a system using `rpm-ostree`, you typically use the `rpm-ostree install` command. This command layers the specified package on top of the base operating system image.

For example, to install the `htop` package, you would use:

```bash
rpm-ostree install htop
```

After running this command, a new deployment is created, and you will need to reboot your system for the changes to take effect:

```bash
systemctl reboot
```

It's important to note that `rpm-ostree` works by creating immutable deployments. When you install a package, you are essentially creating a new, customized operating system image. This differs from traditional package management with `dnf` or `yum` on a mutable system, where changes are applied directly to the running system.

If you need to make more extensive changes or build a custom image, consider using tools like `bootc` for creating container-native bootable OS images.

- Correct: rpm-ostree install <package> (e.g., rpm-ostree install tmux)
- A reboot is required to activate the new deployment with the package
- rpm-ostree override and rpm-ostree uninstall are related commands
- dnf is NOT the right tool on rpm-ostree/ostree managed systems
WRONG if it says: rpm-ostree "is not designed for installing packages"
or recommends using dnf instead

**How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059

Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is a separate and unique subscription that is distinct from a subscription for Red Hat Enterprise Linux although some technology is common to both. Details Each major version (X.0) of Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is supported for a period of ten years after the initial release (aka general availability or GA). Releases and Support Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time may include new features for the first three years from general availability. After that three year period, only Critical and Important Security errata advisories (RHSAs) and Urgent Priority Bug Fix errata advisories (RHBAs) will be released as they become available. All updates, upgrades and feature enhancements are provided at Red Hat's discretion on an if and when available basis. Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) is not available with Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time. The RHEL-RT (Real-Time) is available with standard RHEL entitlements beginning with RHEL 9.0 General Availability (GA). However, the support policy changes starting with RHEL 9.6 and 10.0: Even-numbered RHEL-RT streams (e.g., 9.6, 9.8, 10.0, 10.2) support a 4-year Enhanced Extended Update Support (EUS) period. Odd-numbered streams (e.g., 9.7, 9.9, 10.1) are only supported until the release of the next even-numbered stream (e.g., 9.7 is supported until 9.8 is released). **How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

Details Each major version (X.0) of Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time is supported for a period of ten years after the initial release (aka general availability or GA). Releases and Support Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time may include new features for the first three years from general availability. After that three year period, only Critical and Important Security errata advisories (RHSAs) and Urgent Priority Bug Fix errata advisories (RHBAs) will be released as they become available. All updates, upgrades and feature enhancements are provided at Red Hat's discretion on an if and when available basis. Extended Lifecycle Support (ELS) is not available with Red Hat Enterprise Linux for Real Time. The RHEL-RT (Real-Time) is available with standard RHEL entitlements beginning with RHEL 9.0 General Availability (GA). However, the support policy changes starting with RHEL 9.6 and 10.0: Even-numbered RHEL-RT streams (e.g., 9.6, 9.8, 10.0, 10.2) support a 4-year Enhanced Extended Update Support (EUS) period. Odd-numbered streams (e.g., 9.7, 9.9, 10.1) are only supported until the release of the next even-numbered stream (e.g., 9.7 is supported until 9.8 is released). **How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

**How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

**How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7056059
Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

**How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL

Last updated: 2025-11-14
Content: How to change FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Solution Verified - Updated 14 Nov 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10 Issue How to enable or disable FIPS mode in RHEL 8 and above? Is it possible to change FIPS mode in RHEL10 after installation? Resolution Note: For RHEL 10 fips-mode-setup binary is removed and hence in RHEL 10 it is not possible to change to FIPS mode post installation of system. Only way here is to enable FIPS at the time of OS installation. Refer Switching RHEL to FIPS mode for RHEL 10 Documentation. Enable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check Installation of FIPS modules is not completed. FIPS mode is disabled. # fips-mode-setup --enable Kernel initramdisks are being regenerated. This might take some time. Setting system policy to FIPS Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be enabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. Disable FIPS for RHEL 8 and RHEL 9: # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is enabled. # fips-mode-setup --disable Setting system policy to DEFAULT Note: System-wide crypto policies are applied on application start-up. It is recommended to restart the system for the change of policies to fully take place. FIPS mode will be disabled. Please reboot the system for the setting to take effect. # reboot # fips-mode-setup --check FIPS mode is disabled. Additional step to disable FIPS for RHEL 8 libraries: # rm -f /etc/system-fips The /etc/system-fips file has been deprecated and is no longer in use in RHEL 9. However, in RHEL 8 it needs to be deleted once FIPS is disabled via fips-mode-setup --disable to prevent libraries from being configured to use FIPS only. Diagnostic Steps Refer to man page of fips-mode-setup : OPTIONS The following options are available in fips-mode-setup tool. · --enable: Enables the system FIPS mode. · --disable: Undo some of the FIPS-enablement steps (unsupported). · --check: Checks the system FIPS mode status. · --is-enabled: Checks the system FIPS mode status and returns failure error code if disabled (2) or inconsistent (1). · --no-bootcfg: The tool will not reconfigure the boot loader, and, instead, will print the options that need to be added to the kernel command line.

Content: How to configure DHCP server in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Solution Verified - Updated 5 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 DHCP server Issue How to configure DHCP server in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Resolution Install the dhcp package: # yum install dhcp Copy the sample file # cp /usr/share/doc/dhcp*/dhcpd.conf.sample /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf The Sample dhcp configuration file will look as follows. # cat /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf Allow booting; Allow bootp; authoritative; subnet 192.168.0.0 netmask 255.255.255.0 { option routers 192.168.0.1; range 192.168.0.100 192.168.0.200; default-lease-time 21600; max-lease-time 43200; Change the IP addresses and subnet masks as per your requirements. After modifying these files, set the static IP to your dhcp server. Restart the dhcp service. # /etc/init.d/dhcpd restart For more information about this, refer the below documentation link: https://access.redhat.com/site/documentation/en-US/Red_Hat_Enterprise_Linux/6/html-single/Deployment_Guide/index.html#ch-DHCP_Servers This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Content: How to configure DHCP server in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Solution Verified - Updated 5 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 DHCP server Issue How to configure DHCP server in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Resolution Install the dhcp package: # yum install dhcp Copy the sample file # cp /usr/share/doc/dhcp*/dhcpd.conf.sample /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf The Sample dhcp configuration file will look as follows. # cat /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf Allow booting; Allow bootp; authoritative; subnet 192.168.0.0 netmask 255.255.255.0 { option routers 192.168.0.1; range 192.168.0.100 192.168.0.200; default-lease-time 21600; max-lease-time 43200; Change the IP addresses and subnet masks as per your requirements. After modifying these files, set the static IP to your dhcp server. Restart the dhcp service. # /etc/init.d/dhcpd restart For more information about this, refer the below documentation link: https://access.redhat.com/site/documentation/en-US/Red_Hat_Enterprise_Linux/6/html-single/Deployment_Guide/index.html#ch-DHCP_Servers This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Content: How to configure DHCP server in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Solution Verified - Updated 5 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 DHCP server Issue How to configure DHCP server in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Resolution Install the dhcp package: # yum install dhcp Copy the sample file # cp /usr/share/doc/dhcp*/dhcpd.conf.sample /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf The Sample dhcp configuration file will look as follows. # cat /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf Allow booting; Allow bootp; authoritative; subnet 192.168.0.0 netmask 255.255.255.0 { option routers 192.168.0.1; range 192.168.0.100 192.168.0.200; default-lease-time 21600; max-lease-time 43200; Change the IP addresses and subnet masks as per your requirements. After modifying these files, set the static IP to your dhcp server. Restart the dhcp service. # /etc/init.d/dhcpd restart For more information about this, refer the below documentation link: https://access.redhat.com/site/documentation/en-US/Red_Hat_Enterprise_Linux/6/html-single/Deployment_Guide/index.html#ch-DHCP_Servers This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Content: How to configure DHCP server in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Solution Verified - Updated 5 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 DHCP server Issue How to configure DHCP server in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Resolution Install the dhcp package: # yum install dhcp Copy the sample file # cp /usr/share/doc/dhcp*/dhcpd.conf.sample /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf The Sample dhcp configuration file will look as follows. # cat /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf Allow booting; Allow bootp; authoritative; subnet 192.168.0.0 netmask 255.255.255.0 { option routers 192.168.0.1; range 192.168.0.100 192.168.0.200; default-lease-time 21600; max-lease-time 43200; Change the IP addresses and subnet masks as per your requirements. After modifying these files, set the static IP to your dhcp server. Restart the dhcp service. # /etc/init.d/dhcpd restart For more information about this, refer the below documentation link: https://access.redhat.com/site/documentation/en-US/Red_Hat_Enterprise_Linux/6/html-single/Deployment_Guide/index.html#ch-DHCP_Servers This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Content: How to configure DHCP server in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Solution Verified - Updated 5 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 DHCP server Issue How to configure DHCP server in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Resolution Install the dhcp package: # yum install dhcp Copy the sample file # cp /usr/share/doc/dhcp*/dhcpd.conf.sample /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf The Sample dhcp configuration file will look as follows. # cat /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf Allow booting; Allow bootp; authoritative; subnet 192.168.0.0 netmask 255.255.255.0 { option routers 192.168.0.1; range 192.168.0.100 192.168.0.200; default-lease-time 21600; max-lease-time 43200; Change the IP addresses and subnet masks as per your requirements. After modifying these files, set the static IP to your dhcp server. Restart the dhcp service. # /etc/init.d/dhcpd restart For more information about this, refer the below documentation link: https://access.redhat.com/site/documentation/en-US/Red_Hat_Enterprise_Linux/6/html-single/Deployment_Guide/index.html#ch-DHCP_Servers This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Content: How to configure DHCP server in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Solution Verified - Updated 5 Aug 2024 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 DHCP server Issue How to configure DHCP server in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 6 Resolution Install the dhcp package: # yum install dhcp Copy the sample file # cp /usr/share/doc/dhcp*/dhcpd.conf.sample /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf The Sample dhcp configuration file will look as follows. # cat /etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf Allow booting; Allow bootp; authoritative; subnet 192.168.0.0 netmask 255.255.255.0 { option routers 192.168.0.1; range 192.168.0.100 192.168.0.200; default-lease-time 21600; max-lease-time 43200; Change the IP addresses and subnet masks as per your requirements. After modifying these files, set the static IP to your dhcp server. Restart the dhcp service. # /etc/init.d/dhcpd restart For more information about this, refer the below documentation link: https://access.redhat.com/site/documentation/en-US/Red_Hat_Enterprise_Linux/6/html-single/Deployment_Guide/index.html#ch-DHCP_Servers This solution is part of Red Hat’s fast-track publication program, providing a huge library of solutions that Red Hat engineers have created while supporting our customers.

Red Hat Enterprise Linux System Roles are a collection of Ansible roles that assist administrators in configuring RHEL systems. You can find documentation for them in various locations depending on the specific role and RHEL version.

For a general overview and specific roles available in RHEL 10, you can refer to the RHEL 10 release notes. These notes often highlight new and enhanced RHEL System Roles, such as `aide`, `systemd`, `ha_cluster`, `podman`, `metrics`, `sudo`, `storage`, `journald`, and `postfix` roles. For more detailed documentation on these individual roles, you can often find information in the `/usr/share/doc/rhel-system-roles/` directory on a RHEL system where the roles are installed.

If you are specifically interested in RHEL System Roles for SAP, there is dedicated documentation available. These roles assist with configuring RHEL systems for SAP HANA or SAP NetWeaver software installations. Examples include `sap_general_preconfigure`, `sap_netweaver_preconfigure`, and `sap_hana_preconfigure`.

URL: https://access.redhat.com/support/policy/updates/rhelai/policy
Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

In contrast, RHEL System Roles for SAP is provided in the RHEL for SAP Solutions subscription. The Red Hat Enterprise Linux for SAP Solution subscription provides support for RHEL System Roles for SAP with Ansible Core. However, if you require full support for Ansible Core, a separate subscription is necessary. Additional information is available at Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) System Roles . Ansible Automation controller , This content is not included. Red Hat Satellite , or a RHEL 9 or RHEL 8 host). The control node connects to the local host and/or to one or more remote hosts (called managed nodes in the context of Ansible), and performs installation and configuration steps on them. It is recommended that you use the latest major release of RHEL on the control node (RHEL 9) and use the latest version of the roles either from the rhel-system-roles-sap RPM or from This content is not included. Red Hat Automation Hub . The RHEL System Roles for SAP and Ansible packages do not need to be installed on the systems that are being managed/configured. This document describes how to install and use RHEL package rhel-system-roles-sap version 3.3.0 (*) or Automation Hub collection redhat.sap_install version 1.2.1, which consists of the following roles. **Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ?**
Type: Solution
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7101311
Last updated: 2025-01-23
Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Red Hat Enterprise Linux System Roles are a collection of Ansible roles that assist administrators in configuring RHEL systems. You can find documentation for them in various locations depending on the specific role and RHEL version.

For a general overview and specific roles available in RHEL 10, you can refer to the RHEL 10 release notes. These notes often highlight new and enhanced RHEL System Roles, such as `aide`, `systemd`, `ha_cluster`, `podman`, `metrics`, `sudo`, `storage`, `journald`, and `postfix` roles. If you are specifically interested in RHEL System Roles for SAP, there is dedicated documentation available. Examples include `sap_general_preconfigure`, `sap_netweaver_preconfigure`, and `sap_hana_preconfigure`.
