Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Red Hat Enterprise Linux System Roles are a collection of Ansible roles that assist administrators in configuring RHEL systems. You can find documentation for them in various locations depending on the specific role and RHEL version.

Examples include `sap_general_preconfigure`, `sap_netweaver_preconfigure`, and `sap_hana_preconfigure`.

Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. Type: Documentation
Applicability: RHEL
URL: https://access.redhat.com/support/policy/updates/rhelai/policy
Last updated: 2026-02-24
Content: Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI Support Policy Purpose This document describes how Red Hat provides support for components of Red Hat Enterprise Linux AI (RHEL AI). Support of individual components is limited to use of RHEL AI via the RHEL AI user interface (UI), command line interface (CLI), and application programming interface (API) when deployed via the provided RHEL AI images. We do not support direct use of individual components. Additional software installed by the customer is unsupported as described in the Red Hat support policy on third party software . Specific technologies shipped with RHEL AI are: Granite : an open source, Apache 2 licensed foundation model from IBM. RHEL Image Mode (bootc) : RHEL AI is distributed as a “bootable container” image. These RHEL AI appliance images are provisioned via a kickstart enabled installer (downloaded as an iso, raw, or qcow2 image) onto bare metal or cloud instances. vLLM : A high-throughput and memory-efficient inference and serving engine for LLMs, based on PyTorch. GPU Enablement : Drivers and packages to provide GPU based acceleration. Granite Only the models listed in the official product documentation are supported. Depending on the release version, some models may be released as Technology Preview . Please see RHEL AI documentation for supported models for your version. Granite models are open source with support via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . Use of other models, specifically non-generative chat models, is not supported. Image Mode for RHEL Image mode is supported under GA terms. The Red Hat Container Support Policy outlines policy specific to containers. vLLM vLLM components provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via typical support paths at access.redhat.com . GPU Enablement These packages are distributed by Red Hat in the RHEL AI images. GPU enablement packages provided with RHEL AI are supported only when used by the RHEL AI provided UI, CLI, and API. Support is via the typical support through access.redhat.com . Red Hat will engage vendors as needed for additional support of these packages.

Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... If we switch to Kerberos, which is key-based authentication, it will not support this use case. We would like to know if Red Hat v9 supports NTLM authentication ? Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. RHEL System Roles for SAP development is based on the Content from sap-linuxlab.github.io is not included. SAP LinuxLab upstream project. In contrast, RHEL System Roles for SAP is provided in the RHEL for SAP Solutions subscription. The Red Hat Enterprise Linux for SAP Solution subscription provides support for RHEL System Roles for SAP with Ansible Core. However, if you require full support for Ansible Core, a separate subscription is necessary. Additional information is available at Red Hat Enterprise Linux (RHEL) System Roles . The following RHEL System Roles for SAP are fully supported on control nodes running RHEL 8.4 and later: sap_general_preconfigure sap_netweaver_preconfigure sap_hana_preconfigure sap_hana_install The following RHEL System Roles for SAP are supported as Technology Preview on control nodes running RHEL 8.4 and later: sap_ha_install_hana_hsr sap_ha_pacemaker_cluster sap_swpm (*) The RHEL System Roles for SAP, just like the RHEL System Roles , are installed and run from a central node referred to as the control node (which can be Content from www.ansible.com is not included. Ansible Automation controller , This content is not included. Red Hat Satellite , or a RHEL 9 or RHEL 8 host). The control node connects to the local host and/or to one or more remote hosts (called managed nodes in the context of Ansible), and performs installation and configuration steps on them. It is recommended that you use the latest major release of RHEL on the control node (RHEL 9) and use the latest version of the roles either from the rhel-system-roles-sap RPM or from This content is not included. Red Hat Automation Hub . The RHEL System Roles for SAP and Ansible packages do not need to be installed on the systems that are being managed/configured. This document describes how to install and use RHEL package rhel-system-roles-sap version 3.3.0 (*) or Automation Hub collection redhat.sap_install version 1.2.1, which consists of the following roles. Note Package rhel-system-roles-sap version 3.3.0 does not contain the role sap_swpm . This role is planned to be released in the next version of package rhel-system-roles-sap . Purpose Role Name Description sap_general_preconfigure Perform installation and configuration steps common to SAP NetWeaver and SAP HANA sap_netweaver_preconfigure Perform additional installation and configuration steps for SAP NetWeaver sap_hana_preconfigure Perform additional installation and configuration for SAP HANA sap_hana_install Perform a SAP HANA scale-up or scale-out installation sap_ha_install_hana_hsr Set up SAP HANA System Replication on two nodes sap_ha_pacemaker_cluster Install Pacemaker and configure the cluster and SAP cluster resources sap_swpm Install SAP Software via SWPM Support Status Role Name Support Status Remote Host Management Control Node  [...]

Content: Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? Solution Verified - Updated 23 Jan 2025 Environment Red Hat Enterprise Linux 9 Issue Is NTLM authentication supported on RHEL 9 ? We are using NTLM authentication to connect to our Exchange servers. Our client plans to upgrade the OS from RHEL 8.x to RHEL 9.x. We have learned that NTLM will no longer be supported from version 9 onwards as the package gssntlmssp is deprecated. Is there any other way we can achieve this ? ... Is the package gssntlmssp available on RHEL 9 ? Resolution The gssntlmssp package has been deprecated and removed from RHEL 9. Microsoft also deprecated the NTLM functionality that is being used in windows servers. All versions of NTLM, including LANMAN, NTLMv1, and NTLMv2, are no longer under active feature development and are deprecated. Use of NTLM will continue to work in the next release of Windows Server and the next annual release of Windows. Calls to NTLM should be replaced by calls to Negotiate, which will try to authenticate with Kerberos and only fall back to NTLM when necessary. [Update - November 2024]: NTLMv1 is removed starting in Windows 11, version 24H2 and Windows Server 2025. Source : Microsoft Documentation of Content from learn.microsoft.com is not included. Deprecated features for Windows client Root Cause As Windows New Technology LAN Manager (NTLM) is considered insecure, the gssntlmssp package has been removed. query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 typically involves an in-place upgrade of the operating system using the `Leapp` utility, followed by verifying and potentially adapting your DHCP server configuration. While there isn't a specific Red Hat document detailing the migration of the DHCP *service* itself, the general process for an in-place upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 is as follows:

**1. Plan and Prepare for the Upgrade:**

*   **Review Upgrade Documentation:** Consult the official Red Hat documentation for "Upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10" as this is the recommended and supported method for moving between major RHEL versions. This documentation covers critical steps, prerequisites, limitations, and potential issues.
*   **Backup Your Data and Configuration:** Before any major upgrade, it is crucial to back up all important data and configuration files, especially your DHCP server configuration (e.g., `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf` and related files). This ensures you can restore your system or reconfigure the DHCP server if any issues arise.
*   **Check Application Compatibility:** While the `dhcpd` service is a core component and generally backward compatible, it's good practice to ensure there are no known issues or significant changes in RHEL 10 that might affect your specific configuration.

**2. Perform the In-Place Upgrade:**

*   **Use Leapp Utility:** Red Hat recommends using the `Leapp` utility for in-place upgrades. This tool helps assess your system for potential upgrade issues and performs the necessary changes.
*   **Pre-upgrade Assessment:** Run `Leapp` in pre-upgrade mode to generate a report. This report will identify any blockers or warnings that need to be addressed before proceeding with the actual upgrade. Pay close attention to any warnings related to networking or services.
*   **Resolve Identified Issues:** Address any issues flagged by the `Leapp` pre-upgrade report. This might involve updating certain packages, migrating deprecated components (like `libdb` if your DHCP server relies on it, though this is less common for `dhcpd`), or adjusting configurations.
*   **Execute the Upgrade:** Once all pre-upgrade checks pass, proceed with the `Leapp` upgrade process to transition your RHEL 9 system to RHEL 10.

**3. Post-Upgrade Verification and Configuration:**

*   **Verify DHCP Service:** After the upgrade, verify that the `dhcpd` service is installed, enabled, and running correctly.
    ```bash
    systemctl status dhcpd
    ```
*   **Check Configuration:** Ensure your DHCP configuration files (e.g., `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`) have been preserved or migrated correctly.
*   **Test DHCP Functionality:** Thoroughly test your DHCP server's functionality to ensure it is assigning IP addresses, handling leases, and serving clients as expected.

**Regarding Deprecated Components:**

While `dhcpd` itself is not listed as deprecated, some underlying libraries or components might have changed. For example, `libdb` was deprecated in RHEL 9 and removed in RHEL 10. If your DHCP server or any custom scripts rely on `libdb`, you would need to migrate to a different key-value database such as GDBM. The `libdb-utils` package on RHEL 9 provides a tool for converting to GDBM format.

**Sources:**
- [Upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/upgrading_from_rhel_9_to_rhel_10/index)
- [How to migrate from libdb to a different key-value database](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7106315)

query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 typically involves an in-place upgrade of the operating system using the `Leapp` utility, followed by verifying and potentially adapting your DHCP server configuration. While there isn't a specific Red Hat document detailing the migration of the DHCP *service* itself, the general process for an in-place upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 is as follows:

**1. Plan and Prepare for the Upgrade:**

*   **Review Upgrade Documentation:** Consult the official Red Hat documentation for "Upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10" as this is the recommended and supported method for moving between major RHEL versions. This documentation covers critical steps, prerequisites, limitations, and potential issues.
*   **Backup Your Data and Configuration:** Before any major upgrade, it is crucial to back up all important data and configuration files, especially your DHCP server configuration (e.g., `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf` and related files). This ensures you can restore your system or reconfigure the DHCP server if any issues arise.
*   **Check Application Compatibility:** While the `dhcpd` service is a core component and generally backward compatible, it's good practice to ensure there are no known issues or significant changes in RHEL 10 that might affect your specific configuration.

**2. Perform the In-Place Upgrade:**

*   **Use Leapp Utility:** Red Hat recommends using the `Leapp` utility for in-place upgrades. This tool helps assess your system for potential upgrade issues and performs the necessary changes.
*   **Pre-upgrade Assessment:** Run `Leapp` in pre-upgrade mode to generate a report. This report will identify any blockers or warnings that need to be addressed before proceeding with the actual upgrade. Pay close attention to any warnings related to networking or services.
*   **Resolve Identified Issues:** Address any issues flagged by the `Leapp` pre-upgrade report. This might involve updating certain packages, migrating deprecated components (like `libdb` if your DHCP server relies on it, though this is less common for `dhcpd`), or adjusting configurations.
*   **Execute the Upgrade:** Once all pre-upgrade checks pass, proceed with the `Leapp` upgrade process to transition your RHEL 9 system to RHEL 10.

**3. Post-Upgrade Verification and Configuration:**

*   **Verify DHCP Service:** After the upgrade, verify that the `dhcpd` service is installed, enabled, and running correctly.
    ```bash
    systemctl status dhcpd
    ```
*   **Check Configuration:** Ensure your DHCP configuration files (e.g., `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`) have been preserved or migrated correctly.
*   **Test DHCP Functionality:** Thoroughly test your DHCP server's functionality to ensure it is assigning IP addresses, handling leases, and serving clients as expected.

**Regarding Deprecated Components:**

While `dhcpd` itself is not listed as deprecated, some underlying libraries or components might have changed. For example, `libdb` was deprecated in RHEL 9 and removed in RHEL 10. If your DHCP server or any custom scripts rely on `libdb`, you would need to migrate to a different key-value database such as GDBM. The `libdb-utils` package on RHEL 9 provides a tool for converting to GDBM format.

**Sources:**
- [Upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/upgrading_from_rhel_9_to_rhel_10/index)
- [How to migrate from libdb to a different key-value database](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7106315)

query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 typically involves an in-place upgrade of the operating system using the `Leapp` utility, followed by verifying and potentially adapting your DHCP server configuration. While there isn't a specific Red Hat document detailing the migration of the DHCP *service* itself, the general process for an in-place upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 is as follows:

**1. Plan and Prepare for the Upgrade:**

*   **Review Upgrade Documentation:** Consult the official Red Hat documentation for "Upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10" as this is the recommended and supported method for moving between major RHEL versions. This documentation covers critical steps, prerequisites, limitations, and potential issues.
*   **Backup Your Data and Configuration:** Before any major upgrade, it is crucial to back up all important data and configuration files, especially your DHCP server configuration (e.g., `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf` and related files). This ensures you can restore your system or reconfigure the DHCP server if any issues arise.
*   **Check Application Compatibility:** While the `dhcpd` service is a core component and generally backward compatible, it's good practice to ensure there are no known issues or significant changes in RHEL 10 that might affect your specific configuration.

**2. Perform the In-Place Upgrade:**

*   **Use Leapp Utility:** Red Hat recommends using the `Leapp` utility for in-place upgrades. This tool helps assess your system for potential upgrade issues and performs the necessary changes.
*   **Pre-upgrade Assessment:** Run `Leapp` in pre-upgrade mode to generate a report. This report will identify any blockers or warnings that need to be addressed before proceeding with the actual upgrade. Pay close attention to any warnings related to networking or services.
*   **Resolve Identified Issues:** Address any issues flagged by the `Leapp` pre-upgrade report. This might involve updating certain packages, migrating deprecated components (like `libdb` if your DHCP server relies on it, though this is less common for `dhcpd`), or adjusting configurations.
*   **Execute the Upgrade:** Once all pre-upgrade checks pass, proceed with the `Leapp` upgrade process to transition your RHEL 9 system to RHEL 10.

**3. Post-Upgrade Verification and Configuration:**

*   **Verify DHCP Service:** After the upgrade, verify that the `dhcpd` service is installed, enabled, and running correctly.
    ```bash
    systemctl status dhcpd
    ```
*   **Check Configuration:** Ensure your DHCP configuration files (e.g., `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`) have been preserved or migrated correctly.
*   **Test DHCP Functionality:** Thoroughly test your DHCP server's functionality to ensure it is assigning IP addresses, handling leases, and serving clients as expected.

**Regarding Deprecated Components:**

While `dhcpd` itself is not listed as deprecated, some underlying libraries or components might have changed. For example, `libdb` was deprecated in RHEL 9 and removed in RHEL 10. If your DHCP server or any custom scripts rely on `libdb`, you would need to migrate to a different key-value database such as GDBM. The `libdb-utils` package on RHEL 9 provides a tool for converting to GDBM format.

**Sources:**
- [Upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/upgrading_from_rhel_9_to_rhel_10/index)
- [How to migrate from libdb to a different key-value database](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7106315)

query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 typically involves an in-place upgrade of the operating system using the `Leapp` utility, followed by verifying and potentially adapting your DHCP server configuration. While there isn't a specific Red Hat document detailing the migration of the DHCP *service* itself, the general process for an in-place upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 is as follows:

**1. Plan and Prepare for the Upgrade:**

*   **Review Upgrade Documentation:** Consult the official Red Hat documentation for "Upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10" as this is the recommended and supported method for moving between major RHEL versions. This documentation covers critical steps, prerequisites, limitations, and potential issues.
*   **Backup Your Data and Configuration:** Before any major upgrade, it is crucial to back up all important data and configuration files, especially your DHCP server configuration (e.g., `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf` and related files). This ensures you can restore your system or reconfigure the DHCP server if any issues arise.
*   **Check Application Compatibility:** While the `dhcpd` service is a core component and generally backward compatible, it's good practice to ensure there are no known issues or significant changes in RHEL 10 that might affect your specific configuration.

**2. Perform the In-Place Upgrade:**

*   **Use Leapp Utility:** Red Hat recommends using the `Leapp` utility for in-place upgrades. This tool helps assess your system for potential upgrade issues and performs the necessary changes.
*   **Pre-upgrade Assessment:** Run `Leapp` in pre-upgrade mode to generate a report. This report will identify any blockers or warnings that need to be addressed before proceeding with the actual upgrade. Pay close attention to any warnings related to networking or services.
*   **Resolve Identified Issues:** Address any issues flagged by the `Leapp` pre-upgrade report. This might involve updating certain packages, migrating deprecated components (like `libdb` if your DHCP server relies on it, though this is less common for `dhcpd`), or adjusting configurations.
*   **Execute the Upgrade:** Once all pre-upgrade checks pass, proceed with the `Leapp` upgrade process to transition your RHEL 9 system to RHEL 10.

**3. Post-Upgrade Verification and Configuration:**

*   **Verify DHCP Service:** After the upgrade, verify that the `dhcpd` service is installed, enabled, and running correctly.
    ```bash
    systemctl status dhcpd
    ```
*   **Check Configuration:** Ensure your DHCP configuration files (e.g., `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`) have been preserved or migrated correctly.
*   **Test DHCP Functionality:** Thoroughly test your DHCP server's functionality to ensure it is assigning IP addresses, handling leases, and serving clients as expected.

**Regarding Deprecated Components:**

While `dhcpd` itself is not listed as deprecated, some underlying libraries or components might have changed. For example, `libdb` was deprecated in RHEL 9 and removed in RHEL 10. If your DHCP server or any custom scripts rely on `libdb`, you would need to migrate to a different key-value database such as GDBM. The `libdb-utils` package on RHEL 9 provides a tool for converting to GDBM format.

**Sources:**
- [Upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/upgrading_from_rhel_9_to_rhel_10/index)
- [How to migrate from libdb to a different key-value database](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7106315)

query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 typically involves an in-place upgrade of the operating system using the `Leapp` utility, followed by verifying and potentially adapting your DHCP server configuration. While there isn't a specific Red Hat document detailing the migration of the DHCP *service* itself, the general process for an in-place upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 is as follows:

**1. Plan and Prepare for the Upgrade:**

*   **Review Upgrade Documentation:** Consult the official Red Hat documentation for "Upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10" as this is the recommended and supported method for moving between major RHEL versions. This documentation covers critical steps, prerequisites, limitations, and potential issues.
*   **Backup Your Data and Configuration:** Before any major upgrade, it is crucial to back up all important data and configuration files, especially your DHCP server configuration (e.g., `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf` and related files). This ensures you can restore your system or reconfigure the DHCP server if any issues arise.
*   **Check Application Compatibility:** While the `dhcpd` service is a core component and generally backward compatible, it's good practice to ensure there are no known issues or significant changes in RHEL 10 that might affect your specific configuration.

**2. Perform the In-Place Upgrade:**

*   **Use Leapp Utility:** Red Hat recommends using the `Leapp` utility for in-place upgrades. This tool helps assess your system for potential upgrade issues and performs the necessary changes.
*   **Pre-upgrade Assessment:** Run `Leapp` in pre-upgrade mode to generate a report. This report will identify any blockers or warnings that need to be addressed before proceeding with the actual upgrade. Pay close attention to any warnings related to networking or services.
*   **Resolve Identified Issues:** Address any issues flagged by the `Leapp` pre-upgrade report. This might involve updating certain packages, migrating deprecated components (like `libdb` if your DHCP server relies on it, though this is less common for `dhcpd`), or adjusting configurations.
*   **Execute the Upgrade:** Once all pre-upgrade checks pass, proceed with the `Leapp` upgrade process to transition your RHEL 9 system to RHEL 10.

**3. Post-Upgrade Verification and Configuration:**

*   **Verify DHCP Service:** After the upgrade, verify that the `dhcpd` service is installed, enabled, and running correctly.
    ```bash
    systemctl status dhcpd
    ```
*   **Check Configuration:** Ensure your DHCP configuration files (e.g., `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`) have been preserved or migrated correctly.
*   **Test DHCP Functionality:** Thoroughly test your DHCP server's functionality to ensure it is assigning IP addresses, handling leases, and serving clients as expected.

**Regarding Deprecated Components:**

While `dhcpd` itself is not listed as deprecated, some underlying libraries or components might have changed. For example, `libdb` was deprecated in RHEL 9 and removed in RHEL 10. If your DHCP server or any custom scripts rely on `libdb`, you would need to migrate to a different key-value database such as GDBM. The `libdb-utils` package on RHEL 9 provides a tool for converting to GDBM format.

**Sources:**
- [Upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/upgrading_from_rhel_9_to_rhel_10/index)
- [How to migrate from libdb to a different key-value database](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7106315)

query: How do I migrate my DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10?
response:
Migrating a DHCP server from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 typically involves an in-place upgrade of the operating system using the `Leapp` utility, followed by verifying and potentially adapting your DHCP server configuration. While there isn't a specific Red Hat document detailing the migration of the DHCP *service* itself, the general process for an in-place upgrade from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10 is as follows:

**1. Plan and Prepare for the Upgrade:**

*   **Review Upgrade Documentation:** Consult the official Red Hat documentation for "Upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10" as this is the recommended and supported method for moving between major RHEL versions. This documentation covers critical steps, prerequisites, limitations, and potential issues.
*   **Backup Your Data and Configuration:** Before any major upgrade, it is crucial to back up all important data and configuration files, especially your DHCP server configuration (e.g., `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf` and related files). This ensures you can restore your system or reconfigure the DHCP server if any issues arise.
*   **Check Application Compatibility:** While the `dhcpd` service is a core component and generally backward compatible, it's good practice to ensure there are no known issues or significant changes in RHEL 10 that might affect your specific configuration.

**2. Perform the In-Place Upgrade:**

*   **Use Leapp Utility:** Red Hat recommends using the `Leapp` utility for in-place upgrades. This tool helps assess your system for potential upgrade issues and performs the necessary changes.
*   **Pre-upgrade Assessment:** Run `Leapp` in pre-upgrade mode to generate a report. This report will identify any blockers or warnings that need to be addressed before proceeding with the actual upgrade. Pay close attention to any warnings related to networking or services.
*   **Resolve Identified Issues:** Address any issues flagged by the `Leapp` pre-upgrade report. This might involve updating certain packages, migrating deprecated components (like `libdb` if your DHCP server relies on it, though this is less common for `dhcpd`), or adjusting configurations.
*   **Execute the Upgrade:** Once all pre-upgrade checks pass, proceed with the `Leapp` upgrade process to transition your RHEL 9 system to RHEL 10.

**3. Post-Upgrade Verification and Configuration:**

*   **Verify DHCP Service:** After the upgrade, verify that the `dhcpd` service is installed, enabled, and running correctly.
    ```bash
    systemctl status dhcpd
    ```
*   **Check Configuration:** Ensure your DHCP configuration files (e.g., `/etc/dhcp/dhcpd.conf`) have been preserved or migrated correctly.
*   **Test DHCP Functionality:** Thoroughly test your DHCP server's functionality to ensure it is assigning IP addresses, handling leases, and serving clients as expected.

**Regarding Deprecated Components:**

While `dhcpd` itself is not listed as deprecated, some underlying libraries or components might have changed. For example, `libdb` was deprecated in RHEL 9 and removed in RHEL 10. If your DHCP server or any custom scripts rely on `libdb`, you would need to migrate to a different key-value database such as GDBM. The `libdb-utils` package on RHEL 9 provides a tool for converting to GDBM format.

**Sources:**
- [Upgrading from RHEL 9 to RHEL 10](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/upgrading_from_rhel_9_to_rhel_10/index)
- [How to migrate from libdb to a different key-value database](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7106315)

query: How to set up Kea?
response:
Hello! I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions.

Kea is the new Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) server solution in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, replacing ISC DHCP. It supports DHCPv4, DHCPv6, and Dynamic DNS servers, and offers advantages such as an extensible server solution with module hooks and re-configuration through a REST API.

To set up Kea on RHEL 10, you can follow these general steps:

1.  **Install the Kea DHCP server:**

    ```bash
    dnf install kea
    ```

2.  **Configure the Kea DHCP server:**
    The main configuration file for Kea DHCPv4 is `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf`. You will need to edit this file to define your network interfaces, subnets, IP address pools, and DHCP options.

    Here is an example configuration for `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf` to provide DHCP services for a subnet, including routers and DNS servers. This example assumes your network interface is `enp7s0`, your network ID is `192.168.10.0/24`, the DHCP server IP is `192.168.10.1`, and the subnet range for clients is `192.168.10.100 - 192.168.10.200`.

    ```json
    {
      "Dhcp4": {
        "interfaces-config": {
          "interfaces": [ "enp7s0" ]
        },
        "subnet4": [
          {
            "id": 1,
            "subnet": "192.168.10.0/24",
            "pools": [
              {
                "pool": "192.168.10.100 - 192.168.10.200"
              }
            ],
            "option-data": [
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "routers",
                "code": 3,
                "data": "192.168.10.1"
              },
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "domain-name-servers",
                "code": 6,
                "data": "192.168.10.1"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    }
    ```
    This example also includes basic `client-classes` for PXE booting UEFI and BIOS clients, which might be useful if you're setting up a PXE server.

    You will need to adjust the `interfaces`, `subnet`, `pools`, `data` for `routers`, and `data` for `domain-name-servers` to match your specific network configuration.

3.  **Enable and start the Kea DHCP service:**
    After configuring Kea, you'll need to enable and start the service.

    ```bash
    systemctl enable kea-dhcp4
    systemctl start kea-dhcp4
    ```

4.  **Verify the service status:**
    You can check the status of the Kea DHCPv4 service with:

    ```bash
    systemctl status kea-dhcp4
    ```

For detailed information and advanced configurations, including DHCPv6 and Dynamic DNS, refer to the official Red Hat documentation.

**Sources:**
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10.0 Release Notes](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/10.0_release_notes/index)
- [How to configure/setup a PXE server on RHEL 10 ?](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7126352)

query: How to set up Kea?
response:
Hello! I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions.

Kea is the new Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) server solution in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, replacing ISC DHCP. It supports DHCPv4, DHCPv6, and Dynamic DNS servers, and offers advantages such as an extensible server solution with module hooks and re-configuration through a REST API.

To set up Kea on RHEL 10, you can follow these general steps:

1.  **Install the Kea DHCP server:**

    ```bash
    dnf install kea
    ```

2.  **Configure the Kea DHCP server:**
    The main configuration file for Kea DHCPv4 is `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf`. You will need to edit this file to define your network interfaces, subnets, IP address pools, and DHCP options.

    Here is an example configuration for `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf` to provide DHCP services for a subnet, including routers and DNS servers. This example assumes your network interface is `enp7s0`, your network ID is `192.168.10.0/24`, the DHCP server IP is `192.168.10.1`, and the subnet range for clients is `192.168.10.100 - 192.168.10.200`.

    ```json
    {
      "Dhcp4": {
        "interfaces-config": {
          "interfaces": [ "enp7s0" ]
        },
        "subnet4": [
          {
            "id": 1,
            "subnet": "192.168.10.0/24",
            "pools": [
              {
                "pool": "192.168.10.100 - 192.168.10.200"
              }
            ],
            "option-data": [
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "routers",
                "code": 3,
                "data": "192.168.10.1"
              },
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "domain-name-servers",
                "code": 6,
                "data": "192.168.10.1"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    }
    ```
    This example also includes basic `client-classes` for PXE booting UEFI and BIOS clients, which might be useful if you're setting up a PXE server.

    You will need to adjust the `interfaces`, `subnet`, `pools`, `data` for `routers`, and `data` for `domain-name-servers` to match your specific network configuration.

3.  **Enable and start the Kea DHCP service:**
    After configuring Kea, you'll need to enable and start the service.

    ```bash
    systemctl enable kea-dhcp4
    systemctl start kea-dhcp4
    ```

4.  **Verify the service status:**
    You can check the status of the Kea DHCPv4 service with:

    ```bash
    systemctl status kea-dhcp4
    ```

For detailed information and advanced configurations, including DHCPv6 and Dynamic DNS, refer to the official Red Hat documentation.

**Sources:**
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10.0 Release Notes](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/10.0_release_notes/index)
- [How to configure/setup a PXE server on RHEL 10 ?](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7126352)

query: How to set up Kea?
response:
Hello! I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions.

Kea is the new Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) server solution in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, replacing ISC DHCP. It supports DHCPv4, DHCPv6, and Dynamic DNS servers, and offers advantages such as an extensible server solution with module hooks and re-configuration through a REST API.

To set up Kea on RHEL 10, you can follow these general steps:

1.  **Install the Kea DHCP server:**

    ```bash
    dnf install kea
    ```

2.  **Configure the Kea DHCP server:**
    The main configuration file for Kea DHCPv4 is `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf`. You will need to edit this file to define your network interfaces, subnets, IP address pools, and DHCP options.

    Here is an example configuration for `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf` to provide DHCP services for a subnet, including routers and DNS servers. This example assumes your network interface is `enp7s0`, your network ID is `192.168.10.0/24`, the DHCP server IP is `192.168.10.1`, and the subnet range for clients is `192.168.10.100 - 192.168.10.200`.

    ```json
    {
      "Dhcp4": {
        "interfaces-config": {
          "interfaces": [ "enp7s0" ]
        },
        "subnet4": [
          {
            "id": 1,
            "subnet": "192.168.10.0/24",
            "pools": [
              {
                "pool": "192.168.10.100 - 192.168.10.200"
              }
            ],
            "option-data": [
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "routers",
                "code": 3,
                "data": "192.168.10.1"
              },
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "domain-name-servers",
                "code": 6,
                "data": "192.168.10.1"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    }
    ```
    This example also includes basic `client-classes` for PXE booting UEFI and BIOS clients, which might be useful if you're setting up a PXE server.

    You will need to adjust the `interfaces`, `subnet`, `pools`, `data` for `routers`, and `data` for `domain-name-servers` to match your specific network configuration.

3.  **Enable and start the Kea DHCP service:**
    After configuring Kea, you'll need to enable and start the service.

    ```bash
    systemctl enable kea-dhcp4
    systemctl start kea-dhcp4
    ```

4.  **Verify the service status:**
    You can check the status of the Kea DHCPv4 service with:

    ```bash
    systemctl status kea-dhcp4
    ```

For detailed information and advanced configurations, including DHCPv6 and Dynamic DNS, refer to the official Red Hat documentation.

**Sources:**
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10.0 Release Notes](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/10.0_release_notes/index)
- [How to configure/setup a PXE server on RHEL 10 ?](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7126352)

query: How to set up Kea?
response:
Hello! I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions.

Kea is the new Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) server solution in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, replacing ISC DHCP. It supports DHCPv4, DHCPv6, and Dynamic DNS servers, and offers advantages such as an extensible server solution with module hooks and re-configuration through a REST API.

To set up Kea on RHEL 10, you can follow these general steps:

1.  **Install the Kea DHCP server:**

    ```bash
    dnf install kea
    ```

2.  **Configure the Kea DHCP server:**
    The main configuration file for Kea DHCPv4 is `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf`. You will need to edit this file to define your network interfaces, subnets, IP address pools, and DHCP options.

    Here is an example configuration for `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf` to provide DHCP services for a subnet, including routers and DNS servers. This example assumes your network interface is `enp7s0`, your network ID is `192.168.10.0/24`, the DHCP server IP is `192.168.10.1`, and the subnet range for clients is `192.168.10.100 - 192.168.10.200`.

    ```json
    {
      "Dhcp4": {
        "interfaces-config": {
          "interfaces": [ "enp7s0" ]
        },
        "subnet4": [
          {
            "id": 1,
            "subnet": "192.168.10.0/24",
            "pools": [
              {
                "pool": "192.168.10.100 - 192.168.10.200"
              }
            ],
            "option-data": [
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "routers",
                "code": 3,
                "data": "192.168.10.1"
              },
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "domain-name-servers",
                "code": 6,
                "data": "192.168.10.1"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    }
    ```
    This example also includes basic `client-classes` for PXE booting UEFI and BIOS clients, which might be useful if you're setting up a PXE server.

    You will need to adjust the `interfaces`, `subnet`, `pools`, `data` for `routers`, and `data` for `domain-name-servers` to match your specific network configuration.

3.  **Enable and start the Kea DHCP service:**
    After configuring Kea, you'll need to enable and start the service.

    ```bash
    systemctl enable kea-dhcp4
    systemctl start kea-dhcp4
    ```

4.  **Verify the service status:**
    You can check the status of the Kea DHCPv4 service with:

    ```bash
    systemctl status kea-dhcp4
    ```

For detailed information and advanced configurations, including DHCPv6 and Dynamic DNS, refer to the official Red Hat documentation.

**Sources:**
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10.0 Release Notes](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/10.0_release_notes/index)
- [How to configure/setup a PXE server on RHEL 10 ?](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7126352)

query: How to set up Kea?
response:
Hello! I'm a Red Hat Enterprise Linux expert assistant. I can help you with RHEL-related questions.

Kea is the new Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP) server solution in Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10, replacing ISC DHCP. It supports DHCPv4, DHCPv6, and Dynamic DNS servers, and offers advantages such as an extensible server solution with module hooks and re-configuration through a REST API.

To set up Kea on RHEL 10, you can follow these general steps:

1.  **Install the Kea DHCP server:**

    ```bash
    dnf install kea
    ```

2.  **Configure the Kea DHCP server:**
    The main configuration file for Kea DHCPv4 is `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf`. You will need to edit this file to define your network interfaces, subnets, IP address pools, and DHCP options.

    Here is an example configuration for `/etc/kea/kea-dhcp4.conf` to provide DHCP services for a subnet, including routers and DNS servers. This example assumes your network interface is `enp7s0`, your network ID is `192.168.10.0/24`, the DHCP server IP is `192.168.10.1`, and the subnet range for clients is `192.168.10.100 - 192.168.10.200`.

    ```json
    {
      "Dhcp4": {
        "interfaces-config": {
          "interfaces": [ "enp7s0" ]
        },
        "subnet4": [
          {
            "id": 1,
            "subnet": "192.168.10.0/24",
            "pools": [
              {
                "pool": "192.168.10.100 - 192.168.10.200"
              }
            ],
            "option-data": [
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "routers",
                "code": 3,
                "data": "192.168.10.1"
              },
              {
                "space": "dhcp4",
                "name": "domain-name-servers",
                "code": 6,
                "data": "192.168.10.1"
              }
            ]
          }
        ]
      }
    }
    ```
    This example also includes basic `client-classes` for PXE booting UEFI and BIOS clients, which might be useful if you're setting up a PXE server.

    You will need to adjust the `interfaces`, `subnet`, `pools`, `data` for `routers`, and `data` for `domain-name-servers` to match your specific network configuration.

3.  **Enable and start the Kea DHCP service:**
    After configuring Kea, you'll need to enable and start the service.

    ```bash
    systemctl enable kea-dhcp4
    systemctl start kea-dhcp4
    ```

4.  **Verify the service status:**
    You can check the status of the Kea DHCPv4 service with:

    ```bash
    systemctl status kea-dhcp4
    ```

For detailed information and advanced configurations, including DHCPv6 and Dynamic DNS, refer to the official Red Hat documentation.

**Sources:**
- [Red Hat Enterprise Linux 10.0 Release Notes](https://access.redhat.com/documentation/en-us/red_hat_enterprise_linux/10/html-single/10.0_release_notes/index)
- [How to configure/setup a PXE server on RHEL 10 ?](https://access.redhat.com/solutions/7126352)
